skills_i_can_now_do: Subordinate the minor idea and place the main point in the emphatic final position (the sentence-level syntax/emphasis move)
Recognize that subordination alone isn't enough — the point must also be moved to the end (the two-stage revision insight)

Identify the target sentence vs. near-miss distractors in multiple choice: spot a sentence that subordinates the wrong thing so the main decision is stuck in the although-clause (Step 4, picked B correctly)
Spot a sentence that subordinates correctly but ends on atmospheric trailing detail instead of the point (Step 5, picked B correctly)
Diagnose a flat four-clause 'and' string and pick the right repair (Step 6, picked A correctly)
Rewrite a flat 'and' string with a frame: subordinate the benefit, land the sentence on the cost (Step 7)
Rewrite a weak equal-weight draft by subordinating the 'both matter' truths into an although-clause and placing the point last (Step 8)
Apply the move cold, without a frame, using new source material (pipeline-growth stat) rather than recycling an earlier idea (Step 9)
Transfer the syntax/emphasis move to a second source (water/power), subordinating the apparent-victory idea and landing on the self-defeating loop (Step 11)
Run the 3-question syntax check (equal 'and' clauses gone? point last?) on my own rewrites
Connect the new move to prior claim-level work: recognize the 'and yet...' pivot from Lesson 3 as an instinctive version of subordination + emphatic final position
Add deliberate stylistic touches while keeping the structure correct (dash before the landing, mirrored 'starved/starves' structure)

skills_i_can_now_do: Recognize when repetition is legitimate (a final gate re-running everything) versus padding, and accept it when the lesson is honest about it
Set a section-level effort budget across three FRQ types, weighting by reading load and complexity (40/30/30, protect the third essay)

Identify FRQ type from its tell on a fresh source set (source SET = synthesis) and name the corresponding moves (weave into my own argument, weight backbone vs. supporting sources, don't summarize in sequence)
Spot a percentages-on-different-bases trap in new data before writing (wind's 34.3% capacity factor vs. 10% generation share) and avoid mashing them together
terms_learned: capacity factor vs. share of generation (percentages on different bases)
